  1. Part 4: Visualization
  2. Interactive Dashboards in Quarto

Interactive Dashboards in Quarto

Lesson 14

Learning Objectives

After this lesson, you will be able to:

  • Leverage Plotly’s built-in interactivity (hover, zoom, selection)
  • Create Quarto dashboard layouts
  • Use tabsets and columns for organized presentation
  • Add dropdown filters with Observable JS (optional)
  • Publish interactive reports to GitHub Pages

Audience-Driven Dashboard Design

Different stakeholders in higher education need different dashboards — and knowing your audience determines what you build:

  • Board of Regents / System Administration: High-level KPIs — total compensation spend, salary competitiveness vs. peers, equity gap trends, cost-per-student. Dashboards should be self-explanatory with minimal interaction required. Think value boxes and summary charts.
  • Provost / Vice Chancellors: College-level comparisons, budget-to-actual, year-over-year trends, scenario projections. These users want filters by division and the ability to drill from summary to detail.
  • Department Chairs: Their department’s data — individual salary positioning, FTE allocation, vacancy rates, adjunct reliance. Detail-level tables with sorting and search are essential.
  • HR / Compensation Analysts: Equity dashboards with statistical controls, regression outputs, flagged outliers, and audit-ready data exports.

Dashboard design also connects to the budget request cycle. The most impactful dashboards are released when decision-makers are actively allocating resources — typically in the spring for the next fiscal year. A compensation competitiveness dashboard released in March, when deans are building budget requests, has far more influence than the same dashboard in August after decisions are made. Timing your analysis outputs to institutional decision rhythms is a force multiplier.

Introduction

Quarto combines the best of literate programming (like R Markdown) with modern web interactivity. When you embed Plotly figures in a Quarto document, they retain full interactivity in the rendered HTML.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

# Create sample salary data
np.random.seed(42)
dates = pd.date_range("2021-01", periods=8, freq="6MS")
divisions = ["L&S", "Medicine", "Engineering", "Business"]

records = []
for div in divisions:
    base = {"L&S": 85000, "Medicine": 105000, "Engineering": 95000, "Business": 92000}[div]
    for i, date in enumerate(dates):
        records.append({
            "date": date,
            "division": div,
            "median_salary": base * (1 + i * 0.025) + np.random.normal(0, 2000),
            "headcount": np.random.randint(150, 300),
            "total_fte": np.random.randint(120, 280)
        })

df = pd.DataFrame(records)
df["median_salary"] = df["median_salary"].round(0)
df.head()
TipIn R 
# R Markdown / Quarto with plotly
library(plotly)
p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = date, y = salary)) + geom_line()
ggplotly(p)  # Makes the ggplot interactive

Plotly Interactivity Features

Plotly charts come with built-in interactivity that requires no additional code.

Hover Information

fig = px.line(
    df,
    x="date",
    y="median_salary",
    color="division",
    title="Median Salary Trends by Division",
    labels={"median_salary": "Median Salary ($)", "date": "Date", "division": "Division"}
)

# Customize hover template
fig.update_traces(
    hovertemplate="<b>%{fullData.name}</b><br>" +
                  "Date: %{x|%b %Y}<br>" +
                  "Salary: $%{y:,.0f}<extra></extra>"
)

fig.update_layout(hovermode="x unified")
fig.show()

Zoom and Pan

Users can: - Box zoom: Click and drag to select a region - Pan: Hold shift and drag - Reset: Double-click to reset view - Autoscale: Click the autoscale button in the toolbar

# Enable range slider for time series
fig = px.line(df, x="date", y="median_salary", color="division")

fig.update_layout(
    xaxis=dict(
        rangeslider=dict(visible=True),
        type="date"
    ),
    title="Interactive Time Series with Range Slider"
)

fig.show()

Click Events and Selection

# Scatter plot with selection capabilities
scatter_data = df.copy()
scatter_data["size"] = scatter_data["headcount"] / 10

fig = px.scatter(
    scatter_data,
    x="total_fte",
    y="median_salary",
    color="division",
    size="size",
    hover_data=["date", "headcount"],
    title="FTE vs Median Salary (click to select points)"
)

fig.update_layout(
    dragmode="select"  # Enable box selection
)

fig.show()

Quarto Dashboard Layouts

Quarto provides special syntax for creating dashboard layouts. Create a new file with the .qmd extension and use the dashboard format.

Basic Dashboard Structure

Create a file called salary-dashboard.qmd:

---
title: "UW Salary Dashboard"
format:
  dashboard:
    orientation: columns
    nav-buttons: [github]
---

Rows and Columns

Use markdown headers to define layout:

---
title: "Salary Dashboard"
format: dashboard
---

## Row {height=70%}

```{python}
# This chart takes 70% of vertical space
fig = px.line(df, x="date", y="median_salary", color="division")
fig.show()
```

## Row {height=30%}

### Column

```{python}
# Bottom left
fig = px.bar(df[df["date"] == df["date"].max()], x="division", y="median_salary")
fig.show()
```

### Column

```{python}
# Bottom right
fig = px.box(df, x="division", y="median_salary")
fig.show()
```

Value Boxes

Display key metrics prominently:

## Row {height=20%}

```{python}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Total Employees"
dict(
    value = f"{df['headcount'].sum():,}",
    icon = "people",
    color = "primary"
)
```

```{python}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Avg Salary"
dict(
    value = f"${df['median_salary'].mean():,.0f}",
    icon = "currency-dollar",
    color = "success"
)
```

Tabsets and Columns

Tabsets organize content without cluttering the page:

Creating Tabsets

::: {.panel-tabset}

## Overview

```{python}
fig = px.line(df, x="date", y="median_salary", color="division",
              title="All Divisions")
fig.show()
```

## By Division

```{python}
fig = make_subplots(rows=2, cols=2, subplot_titles=divisions)
for i, div in enumerate(divisions):
    row, col = divmod(i, 2)
    div_data = df[df["division"] == div]
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(x=div_data["date"], y=div_data["median_salary"], name=div),
        row=row+1, col=col+1
    )
fig.update_layout(height=500, showlegend=False)
fig.show()
```

## Data Table

```{python}
from IPython.display import display
display(df.head(20))
```

:::

Let’s see this in action:

  • Overview
  • By Division
  • Summary Stats
fig = px.line(df, x="date", y="median_salary", color="division",
              title="Salary Trends - All Divisions")
fig.update_layout(legend=dict(orientation="h", y=-0.2))
fig.show()
fig = make_subplots(rows=2, cols=2, subplot_titles=divisions)
for i, div in enumerate(divisions):
    row, col = divmod(i, 2)
    div_data = df[df["division"] == div]
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(x=div_data["date"], y=div_data["median_salary"],
                   name=div, mode="lines+markers"),
        row=row+1, col=col+1
    )
fig.update_layout(height=500, showlegend=False)
fig.update_yaxes(tickformat="$,.0f")
fig.show()
summary = df.groupby("division").agg(
    avg_salary=("median_salary", "mean"),
    min_salary=("median_salary", "min"),
    max_salary=("median_salary", "max"),
    total_headcount=("headcount", "sum")
).round(0)

summary

Multi-Column Layouts

# Left column
fig1 = px.bar(
    df[df["date"] == df["date"].max()],
    x="division",
    y="median_salary",
    title="Current Median Salaries",
    color="division"
)
fig1.update_layout(showlegend=False)
fig1.show()

# Right column
fig2 = px.pie(
    df[df["date"] == df["date"].max()],
    values="headcount",
    names="division",
    title="Headcount Distribution"
)
fig2.show()

Adding Filters (Observable JS)

For truly interactive dashboards, you can add filters using Observable JS (ojs). This requires no server—it runs in the browser.

Basic Dropdown Filter

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof selected_division = Inputs.select(
  ["All", "L&S", "Medicine", "Engineering", "Business"],
  {label: "Select Division:", value: "All"}
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
filtered_data = {
  const data = transpose(salary_data);
  if (selected_division === "All") return data;
  return data.filter(d => d.division === selected_division);
}
```

```{python}
#| output: false
# Make data available to OJS
ojs_define(salary_data = df.to_dict('records'))
```

Linked Charts with OJS

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// Division selector
viewof division = Inputs.select(
  ["All Divisions", ...new Set(salary_data.map(d => d.division))],
  {label: "Division:"}
)

// Date range slider
viewof date_range = Inputs.range(
  [new Date("2021-01-01"), new Date("2024-07-01")],
  {step: 86400000 * 30, label: "Date Range:"}
)
```

When working with OJS in Quarto, use PyCharm’s split view to see your code and quarto preview output side-by-side. The preview will hot-reload as you save changes.

Creating Interactive Tables

For searchable, sortable tables, use the itables library:

# Install: pip install itables
from itables import show

# Display interactive table
show(df, scrollY="300px", scrollCollapse=True, paging=False)

Or use Quarto’s built-in table formatting:

# Create a formatted summary table
latest = df[df["date"] == df["date"].max()].copy()
latest["median_salary"] = latest["median_salary"].apply(lambda x: f"${x:,.0f}")
latest["date"] = latest["date"].dt.strftime("%b %Y")

latest[["division", "date", "median_salary", "headcount", "total_fte"]]

Publishing to GitHub Pages

Step 1: Initialize Git Repository

git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial dashboard commit"

Step 2: Create GitHub Repository

gh repo create my-salary-dashboard --public --source=. --push

Step 3: Publish with Quarto

quarto publish gh-pages

This command: 1. Renders your Quarto project 2. Creates/updates the gh-pages branch 3. Pushes to GitHub 4. Configures GitHub Pages automatically

Step 4: Access Your Dashboard

Your dashboard will be available at: https://yourusername.github.io/my-salary-dashboard/

Add a PyCharm run configuration for publishing: 1. Run → Edit Configurations → Add New → Shell Script 2. Script: quarto publish gh-pages --no-prompt 3. Now you can publish with one click!

Automating with GitHub Actions

Create .github/workflows/publish.yml:

name: Publish Dashboard

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  build-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install pandas numpy plotly

      - name: Setup Quarto
        uses: quarto-dev/quarto-actions/setup@v2

      - name: Publish
        uses: quarto-dev/quarto-actions/publish@v2
        with:
          target: gh-pages
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

Exercise

Exercise 14: Build a Salary Explorer Dashboard

Create a Quarto dashboard that includes:

  1. A tabset with “Overview” and “By Division” tabs
  2. Interactive line chart of salary trends with hover details
  3. A summary table showing key metrics by division
  4. Two-column layout comparing headcount vs FTE trends
  5. At least one value box showing a key metric
NoteSolution

Create a file salary-explorer.qmd:

---
title: "UW Salary Explorer"
format:
  dashboard:
    orientation: rows
    theme: cosmo
---

```{python}
#| include: false
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go

# Generate sample data
np.random.seed(42)
dates = pd.date_range("2021-01", periods=8, freq="6MS")
divisions = ["L&S", "Medicine", "Engineering", "Business"]

records = []
for div in divisions:
    base = {"L&S": 85000, "Medicine": 105000, "Engineering": 95000, "Business": 92000}[div]
    for i, date in enumerate(dates):
        records.append({
            "date": date,
            "division": div,
            "median_salary": base * (1 + i * 0.025) + np.random.normal(0, 2000),
            "headcount": np.random.randint(150, 300),
            "total_fte": np.random.randint(120, 280)
        })

df = pd.DataFrame(records)
df["median_salary"] = df["median_salary"].round(0)
```

## Row {height=15%}

```{python}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Total Headcount (Latest)"
latest = df[df["date"] == df["date"].max()]
dict(value=f"{latest['headcount'].sum():,}", icon="people", color="primary")
```

```{python}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Average Median Salary"
dict(value=f"${latest['median_salary'].mean():,.0f}", icon="currency-dollar", color="success")
```

```{python}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Total FTE"
dict(value=f"{latest['total_fte'].sum():,}", icon="briefcase", color="info")
```

## Row {height=85%}

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Overview

```{python}
fig = px.line(
    df, x="date", y="median_salary", color="division",
    title="Median Salary Trends by Division",
    labels={"median_salary": "Median Salary ($)", "date": "Date"}
)
fig.update_traces(hovertemplate="%{y:$,.0f}")
fig.update_layout(
    legend=dict(orientation="h", y=-0.15),
    hovermode="x unified"
)
fig.show()
```

### By Division

```{python}
#| layout-ncol: 2

# Headcount trends
fig1 = px.line(df, x="date", y="headcount", color="division", title="Headcount Trends")
fig1.update_layout(showlegend=False)
fig1.show()

# FTE trends
fig2 = px.line(df, x="date", y="total_fte", color="division", title="FTE Trends")
fig2.update_layout(showlegend=True, legend=dict(orientation="h", y=-0.2))
fig2.show()
```

### Summary Table

```{python}
summary = df.groupby("division").agg(
    latest_salary=("median_salary", "last"),
    salary_change=("median_salary", lambda x: x.iloc[-1] - x.iloc[0]),
    avg_headcount=("headcount", "mean"),
    avg_fte=("total_fte", "mean")
).round(0)

summary["salary_change_pct"] = (summary["salary_change"] / (summary["latest_salary"] - summary["salary_change"]) * 100).round(1)

summary
```

:::

Summary

You’ve learned to create interactive dashboards:

  • Plotly Interactivity: Hover, zoom, pan, and selection built-in
  • Dashboard Layouts: Rows, columns, and value boxes
  • Tabsets: Organize multiple views without clutter
  • OJS Filters: Client-side interactivity without a server
  • Publishing: Deploy to GitHub Pages with one command

Key takeaways: - Plotly + Quarto = interactive reports with no server required - Use tabsets to present multiple views of the same data - Value boxes highlight key metrics at a glance - GitHub Pages provides free hosting for your dashboards

Next Steps

Continue to Lesson 15: Working with APIs to learn how to fetch data programmatically.

Source Code
---
title: "Interactive Dashboards in Quarto"
subtitle: "Lesson 14"
---

::: {.learning-objectives}
#### Learning Objectives

After this lesson, you will be able to:

- Leverage Plotly's built-in interactivity (hover, zoom, selection)
- Create Quarto dashboard layouts
- Use tabsets and columns for organized presentation
- Add dropdown filters with Observable JS (optional)
- Publish interactive reports to GitHub Pages
:::

::: {.concept-box}
#### Audience-Driven Dashboard Design

Different stakeholders in higher education need different dashboards — and knowing your audience determines what you build:

- **Board of Regents / System Administration**: High-level KPIs — total compensation spend, salary competitiveness vs. peers, equity gap trends, cost-per-student. Dashboards should be self-explanatory with minimal interaction required. Think value boxes and summary charts.
- **Provost / Vice Chancellors**: College-level comparisons, budget-to-actual, year-over-year trends, scenario projections. These users want filters by division and the ability to drill from summary to detail.
- **Department Chairs**: Their department's data — individual salary positioning, FTE allocation, vacancy rates, adjunct reliance. Detail-level tables with sorting and search are essential.
- **HR / Compensation Analysts**: Equity dashboards with statistical controls, regression outputs, flagged outliers, and audit-ready data exports.

Dashboard design also connects to the **budget request cycle**. The most impactful dashboards are released when decision-makers are actively allocating resources — typically in the spring for the next fiscal year. A compensation competitiveness dashboard released in March, when deans are building budget requests, has far more influence than the same dashboard in August after decisions are made. Timing your analysis outputs to institutional decision rhythms is a force multiplier.
:::

## Introduction

Quarto combines the best of literate programming (like R Markdown) with modern web interactivity. When you embed Plotly figures in a Quarto document, they retain full interactivity in the rendered HTML.

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

# Create sample salary data
np.random.seed(42)
dates = pd.date_range("2021-01", periods=8, freq="6MS")
divisions = ["L&S", "Medicine", "Engineering", "Business"]

records = []
for div in divisions:
    base = {"L&S": 85000, "Medicine": 105000, "Engineering": 95000, "Business": 92000}[div]
    for i, date in enumerate(dates):
        records.append({
            "date": date,
            "division": div,
            "median_salary": base * (1 + i * 0.025) + np.random.normal(0, 2000),
            "headcount": np.random.randint(150, 300),
            "total_fte": np.random.randint(120, 280)
        })

df = pd.DataFrame(records)
df["median_salary"] = df["median_salary"].round(0)
df.head()
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## In R
```r
# R Markdown / Quarto with plotly
library(plotly)
p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = date, y = salary)) + geom_line()
ggplotly(p)  # Makes the ggplot interactive
```
:::

## Plotly Interactivity Features

Plotly charts come with built-in interactivity that requires no additional code.

### Hover Information

```{python}
fig = px.line(
    df,
    x="date",
    y="median_salary",
    color="division",
    title="Median Salary Trends by Division",
    labels={"median_salary": "Median Salary ($)", "date": "Date", "division": "Division"}
)

# Customize hover template
fig.update_traces(
    hovertemplate="<b>%{fullData.name}</b><br>" +
                  "Date: %{x|%b %Y}<br>" +
                  "Salary: $%{y:,.0f}<extra></extra>"
)

fig.update_layout(hovermode="x unified")
fig.show()
```

### Zoom and Pan

Users can:
- **Box zoom**: Click and drag to select a region
- **Pan**: Hold shift and drag
- **Reset**: Double-click to reset view
- **Autoscale**: Click the autoscale button in the toolbar

```{python}
# Enable range slider for time series
fig = px.line(df, x="date", y="median_salary", color="division")

fig.update_layout(
    xaxis=dict(
        rangeslider=dict(visible=True),
        type="date"
    ),
    title="Interactive Time Series with Range Slider"
)

fig.show()
```

### Click Events and Selection

```{python}
# Scatter plot with selection capabilities
scatter_data = df.copy()
scatter_data["size"] = scatter_data["headcount"] / 10

fig = px.scatter(
    scatter_data,
    x="total_fte",
    y="median_salary",
    color="division",
    size="size",
    hover_data=["date", "headcount"],
    title="FTE vs Median Salary (click to select points)"
)

fig.update_layout(
    dragmode="select"  # Enable box selection
)

fig.show()
```

## Quarto Dashboard Layouts

Quarto provides special syntax for creating dashboard layouts. Create a new file with the `.qmd` extension and use the `dashboard` format.

### Basic Dashboard Structure

Create a file called `salary-dashboard.qmd`:

```yaml
---
title: "UW Salary Dashboard"
format:
  dashboard:
    orientation: columns
    nav-buttons: [github]
---
```

### Rows and Columns

Use markdown headers to define layout:

````markdown
---
title: "Salary Dashboard"
format: dashboard
---

## Row {height=70%}

```{python}
# This chart takes 70% of vertical space
fig = px.line(df, x="date", y="median_salary", color="division")
fig.show()
```

## Row {height=30%}

### Column

```{python}
# Bottom left
fig = px.bar(df[df["date"] == df["date"].max()], x="division", y="median_salary")
fig.show()
```

### Column

```{python}
# Bottom right
fig = px.box(df, x="division", y="median_salary")
fig.show()
```
````

### Value Boxes

Display key metrics prominently:

````markdown
## Row {height=20%}

```{python}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Total Employees"
dict(
    value = f"{df['headcount'].sum():,}",
    icon = "people",
    color = "primary"
)
```

```{python}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Avg Salary"
dict(
    value = f"${df['median_salary'].mean():,.0f}",
    icon = "currency-dollar",
    color = "success"
)
```
````

## Tabsets and Columns

Tabsets organize content without cluttering the page:

### Creating Tabsets

````markdown
::: {.panel-tabset}

## Overview

```{python}
fig = px.line(df, x="date", y="median_salary", color="division",
              title="All Divisions")
fig.show()
```

## By Division

```{python}
fig = make_subplots(rows=2, cols=2, subplot_titles=divisions)
for i, div in enumerate(divisions):
    row, col = divmod(i, 2)
    div_data = df[df["division"] == div]
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(x=div_data["date"], y=div_data["median_salary"], name=div),
        row=row+1, col=col+1
    )
fig.update_layout(height=500, showlegend=False)
fig.show()
```

## Data Table

```{python}
from IPython.display import display
display(df.head(20))
```

:::
````

Let's see this in action:

::: {.panel-tabset}

## Overview

```{python}
fig = px.line(df, x="date", y="median_salary", color="division",
              title="Salary Trends - All Divisions")
fig.update_layout(legend=dict(orientation="h", y=-0.2))
fig.show()
```

## By Division

```{python}
fig = make_subplots(rows=2, cols=2, subplot_titles=divisions)
for i, div in enumerate(divisions):
    row, col = divmod(i, 2)
    div_data = df[df["division"] == div]
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(x=div_data["date"], y=div_data["median_salary"],
                   name=div, mode="lines+markers"),
        row=row+1, col=col+1
    )
fig.update_layout(height=500, showlegend=False)
fig.update_yaxes(tickformat="$,.0f")
fig.show()
```

## Summary Stats

```{python}
summary = df.groupby("division").agg(
    avg_salary=("median_salary", "mean"),
    min_salary=("median_salary", "min"),
    max_salary=("median_salary", "max"),
    total_headcount=("headcount", "sum")
).round(0)

summary
```

:::

### Multi-Column Layouts

```{python}
#| layout-ncol: 2

# Left column
fig1 = px.bar(
    df[df["date"] == df["date"].max()],
    x="division",
    y="median_salary",
    title="Current Median Salaries",
    color="division"
)
fig1.update_layout(showlegend=False)
fig1.show()

# Right column
fig2 = px.pie(
    df[df["date"] == df["date"].max()],
    values="headcount",
    names="division",
    title="Headcount Distribution"
)
fig2.show()
```

## Adding Filters (Observable JS)

For truly interactive dashboards, you can add filters using Observable JS (ojs). This requires no server—it runs in the browser.

### Basic Dropdown Filter

````markdown
```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof selected_division = Inputs.select(
  ["All", "L&S", "Medicine", "Engineering", "Business"],
  {label: "Select Division:", value: "All"}
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
filtered_data = {
  const data = transpose(salary_data);
  if (selected_division === "All") return data;
  return data.filter(d => d.division === selected_division);
}
```

```{python}
#| output: false
# Make data available to OJS
ojs_define(salary_data = df.to_dict('records'))
```
````

### Linked Charts with OJS

````markdown
```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// Division selector
viewof division = Inputs.select(
  ["All Divisions", ...new Set(salary_data.map(d => d.division))],
  {label: "Division:"}
)

// Date range slider
viewof date_range = Inputs.range(
  [new Date("2021-01-01"), new Date("2024-07-01")],
  {step: 86400000 * 30, label: "Date Range:"}
)
```
````

::: {.pycharm-tip}
When working with OJS in Quarto, use PyCharm's split view to see your code and `quarto preview` output side-by-side. The preview will hot-reload as you save changes.
:::

## Creating Interactive Tables

For searchable, sortable tables, use the `itables` library:

```{python}
#| eval: false
# Install: pip install itables
from itables import show

# Display interactive table
show(df, scrollY="300px", scrollCollapse=True, paging=False)
```

Or use Quarto's built-in table formatting:

```{python}
# Create a formatted summary table
latest = df[df["date"] == df["date"].max()].copy()
latest["median_salary"] = latest["median_salary"].apply(lambda x: f"${x:,.0f}")
latest["date"] = latest["date"].dt.strftime("%b %Y")

latest[["division", "date", "median_salary", "headcount", "total_fte"]]
```

## Publishing to GitHub Pages

### Step 1: Initialize Git Repository

```bash
git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial dashboard commit"
```

### Step 2: Create GitHub Repository

```bash
gh repo create my-salary-dashboard --public --source=. --push
```

### Step 3: Publish with Quarto

```bash
quarto publish gh-pages
```

This command:
1. Renders your Quarto project
2. Creates/updates the `gh-pages` branch
3. Pushes to GitHub
4. Configures GitHub Pages automatically

### Step 4: Access Your Dashboard

Your dashboard will be available at:
`https://yourusername.github.io/my-salary-dashboard/`

::: {.pycharm-tip}
Add a PyCharm run configuration for publishing:
1. Run → Edit Configurations → Add New → Shell Script
2. Script: `quarto publish gh-pages --no-prompt`
3. Now you can publish with one click!
:::

### Automating with GitHub Actions

Create `.github/workflows/publish.yml`:

```yaml
name: Publish Dashboard

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  build-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install pandas numpy plotly

      - name: Setup Quarto
        uses: quarto-dev/quarto-actions/setup@v2

      - name: Publish
        uses: quarto-dev/quarto-actions/publish@v2
        with:
          target: gh-pages
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
```

## Exercise {#exercise}

::: {.exercise-box}
#### Exercise 14: Build a Salary Explorer Dashboard

Create a Quarto dashboard that includes:

1. A tabset with "Overview" and "By Division" tabs
2. Interactive line chart of salary trends with hover details
3. A summary table showing key metrics by division
4. Two-column layout comparing headcount vs FTE trends
5. At least one value box showing a key metric

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Solution

Create a file `salary-explorer.qmd`:

````markdown
---
title: "UW Salary Explorer"
format:
  dashboard:
    orientation: rows
    theme: cosmo
---

```{python}
#| include: false
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go

# Generate sample data
np.random.seed(42)
dates = pd.date_range("2021-01", periods=8, freq="6MS")
divisions = ["L&S", "Medicine", "Engineering", "Business"]

records = []
for div in divisions:
    base = {"L&S": 85000, "Medicine": 105000, "Engineering": 95000, "Business": 92000}[div]
    for i, date in enumerate(dates):
        records.append({
            "date": date,
            "division": div,
            "median_salary": base * (1 + i * 0.025) + np.random.normal(0, 2000),
            "headcount": np.random.randint(150, 300),
            "total_fte": np.random.randint(120, 280)
        })

df = pd.DataFrame(records)
df["median_salary"] = df["median_salary"].round(0)
```

## Row {height=15%}

```{python}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Total Headcount (Latest)"
latest = df[df["date"] == df["date"].max()]
dict(value=f"{latest['headcount'].sum():,}", icon="people", color="primary")
```

```{python}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Average Median Salary"
dict(value=f"${latest['median_salary'].mean():,.0f}", icon="currency-dollar", color="success")
```

```{python}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Total FTE"
dict(value=f"{latest['total_fte'].sum():,}", icon="briefcase", color="info")
```

## Row {height=85%}

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Overview

```{python}
fig = px.line(
    df, x="date", y="median_salary", color="division",
    title="Median Salary Trends by Division",
    labels={"median_salary": "Median Salary ($)", "date": "Date"}
)
fig.update_traces(hovertemplate="%{y:$,.0f}")
fig.update_layout(
    legend=dict(orientation="h", y=-0.15),
    hovermode="x unified"
)
fig.show()
```

### By Division

```{python}
#| layout-ncol: 2

# Headcount trends
fig1 = px.line(df, x="date", y="headcount", color="division", title="Headcount Trends")
fig1.update_layout(showlegend=False)
fig1.show()

# FTE trends
fig2 = px.line(df, x="date", y="total_fte", color="division", title="FTE Trends")
fig2.update_layout(showlegend=True, legend=dict(orientation="h", y=-0.2))
fig2.show()
```

### Summary Table

```{python}
summary = df.groupby("division").agg(
    latest_salary=("median_salary", "last"),
    salary_change=("median_salary", lambda x: x.iloc[-1] - x.iloc[0]),
    avg_headcount=("headcount", "mean"),
    avg_fte=("total_fte", "mean")
).round(0)

summary["salary_change_pct"] = (summary["salary_change"] / (summary["latest_salary"] - summary["salary_change"]) * 100).round(1)

summary
```

:::
````
:::
:::

## Summary

You've learned to create interactive dashboards:

- **Plotly Interactivity**: Hover, zoom, pan, and selection built-in
- **Dashboard Layouts**: Rows, columns, and value boxes
- **Tabsets**: Organize multiple views without clutter
- **OJS Filters**: Client-side interactivity without a server
- **Publishing**: Deploy to GitHub Pages with one command

Key takeaways:
- Plotly + Quarto = interactive reports with no server required
- Use tabsets to present multiple views of the same data
- Value boxes highlight key metrics at a glance
- GitHub Pages provides free hosting for your dashboards

## Next Steps

Continue to [Lesson 15: Working with APIs](15-working-with-apis.qmd) to learn how to fetch data programmatically.
 

Built with Quarto | Data: UW-Madison Public Salary Records

  • Edit this page
  • Report an issue
